title: Moss and the Starry Night Sky
author: Fablewick
lesson: Asking questions is its own kind of answer.
summary: Moss the hedgehog has questions about the sky. Tonight, Moss is going to ask them.

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Moss was a small hedgehog with one great big habit.

Moss looked up.

Up at the clouds. Up at the leaves. Up, up, up at the night.

And oh, the night! The night was full of stars. Stars and stars and stars.

"What are stars?" said Moss.

So Moss went to ask the old owl.

"Stars are far away," said the owl. "Far, far away. And old. Very, very old."

"How old?" said Moss.

"Older than me. Older than this tree. Older than the whole forest."

Moss sat in the cool grass and thought and thought.

"Are the stars looking back at me?" said Moss.

The owl blinked, slow as the moon.

"I do not know," said the owl. "What a good question."

"Will you tell me when you find out?"

The owl smiled an owl smile. "Some questions stay questions. That is what makes them good ones."

Moss did not quite understand. Not yet.

But Moss looked up, and the stars looked, maybe, just maybe, a little closer than before.

Moss waved one small paw at the sky.

Just in case.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Moss was a small hedgehog who lived under the roots of an old apple tree, and Moss had one great big habit: Moss looked up.

In the morning, Moss looked up at the clouds. In the afternoon, Moss looked up at the leaves. But at night — oh, at night — Moss looked up at the stars. The deep, dark sky was spilled all over with them, like someone had tipped a whole jar of light.

And Moss had questions. So many questions.

One quiet evening, Moss waddled across the meadow to the old owl, who sat on the lowest branch of a birch tree, thinking owl thoughts.

"Owl," said Moss, "what are stars?"

The owl turned its great round head, slow as the moon. "Stars," said the owl, "are very far away. And very, very old."

"How far?"

"Farther than you could walk in a hundred lifetimes."

"How old?"

"Older than me. Older than this birch. Older, maybe, than the whole forest. Some of those stars have been shining so long that their light is only just now arriving."

Moss sat down in the cool grass to think. A ribbon of moonlight slid across Moss's spines.

"Owl," said Moss at last, "are the stars looking back at me?"

The owl was quiet for a long, long time. Then the owl blinked, once, slowly.

"I do not know," said the owl. "That is an excellent question. I do not have an answer."

"Will you tell me when you find out?"

The owl made a soft sound, almost a laugh.

"Moss," said the owl, "some questions stay questions. You don't ask them to get an answer. You ask them because the asking is the good part. The asking is what makes you look up."

Moss thought about that. Moss thought hard.

Then Moss looked up, and there were so many stars. More stars than Moss could count. More stars than leaves on the apple tree. More stars than words Moss knew.

And looking up felt good. It made Moss feel small, but not sad-small. Small the way the moon is small in a giant sky. Small the way one bright thing is small in a great, kind dark.

Moss lifted one small paw and waved at the stars.

Just in case.

The stars did not wave back. But they kept on shining, steady and bright, the way they had been shining since before anyone could remember.

That was enough for tonight.

Moss waddled home under the apple tree and dreamed, for the very first time, of a road made of stars.

And in the morning, Moss decided: there would be more questions tomorrow.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Moss was a small hedgehog who lived in a snug burrow beneath the roots of an old apple tree, right where the forest ended and the meadow began. And Moss was, by hedgehog standards, a little unusual. Most hedgehogs look down, for beetles, for berries, for soft places to step. Moss looked up.

In the morning, while the rest of the burrow snored, Moss watched the light come down through the leaves in long green slants. In the afternoon, Moss watched the clouds change shape: into dogs, into ships, into slower clouds. In the evening, Moss watched the sky turn pink, then violet, then a deep and serious blue.

But the night — the deep, dark, star-spilled night — was what Moss loved best of all. Moss had, by Moss's own careful count, exactly four hundred and twelve questions about the night sky. And Moss had never asked a single one out loud.

Tonight, that was going to change.

It was a quiet evening. The stars were unusually thick, and the moon was a thin silver curl, like a clipping from something enormous. Moss waddled across the meadow to the white-barked birch tree where the old owl always sat, on the lowest branch, looking exactly like a small feathered thought.

"Owl," said Moss, gathering up every last scrap of courage. "I have a question."

The owl turned its enormous round head, slowly, the way owls do. "Then you may ask it."

"What are stars?"

The owl was quiet for a moment, choosing words the way you might choose stones for crossing a river.

"Stars," said the owl, "are very, very far away. Farther than anyone has ever traveled: no owl, no hedgehog, no anyone. And they are very, very old. Some of the stars you are looking at right now have been shining for so long that their light is only just arriving. The star itself may not even be there anymore. You are seeing where it used to be."

Moss sat down in the cool grass to think about that. It was the kind of thought that needed sitting down for. A ribbon of moonlight slid across Moss's spines and made them shine like wet grass.

"Owl," said Moss at last, in a careful voice. "Are the stars looking back at me?"

The owl did not answer right away. The owl blinked once, slowly. Its eyes were yellow and bright and seemed to hold a great deal of patient time.

"That," said the owl, "is a very good question."

"What's the answer?"

"I do not know," said the owl. "I have lived a long while. I have asked a great many things. That one, I have never found out."

Moss felt, for a small moment, disappointed. Moss had always imagined the owl knew everything, the way trees know how to be trees and rivers know how to be rivers. But the owl did not look disappointed at all. The owl looked, if anything, pleased.

"Will you tell me," said Moss, "if you ever find out?"

The owl made a soft, low sound that was almost a laugh.

"Moss," said the owl, "some questions stay questions. They never turn into answers, and that is not a sad thing. That is the question's whole job. A question that stays a question keeps you looking. It keeps you wondering. It keeps you small in a good way and curious in a wide way. The world is bigger than any answer, little one. The asking is how we touch the bigness."

Moss was not at all sure Moss understood.

But Moss looked back up at the sky, and there were so many stars. More than Moss could count, more than there were leaves on the apple tree, more than there were words Moss knew. The night was enormous. The night did not mind whether Moss understood it. It just went on shining, steady and bright, the way it had been shining since long before there were any hedgehogs to look up at it.

And it was true, Moss thought: even without an answer, looking up felt good. It felt like a kind of belonging. It made Moss feel small in a way that wasn't sad, small the way the moon is small in a giant sky, the way one bright thing is small in a vast and patient dark.

Moss lifted one small paw and waved at the stars.

Just in case.

The stars, as far as Moss could tell, did not wave back. They went on doing what they had always done: being there, and being old, and being beautiful.

That, Moss decided, was enough for tonight.

Moss waddled home across the cool grass, curled up in the snug burrow, and dreamed, for the very first time, of a road made of stars — a long pale road that went on and on and never once needed to arrive anywhere.

In the morning, Moss did the count again. Four hundred and seven questions left, by Moss's count, and a strong feeling that more were already on the way.
